description: 'ritako' is the whole demand of work carried over an interval. It includes the amount and intensity of the work together with the interruption or coordination it brings. Two people with the same task count can carry different workloads, and the body may answer each one differently.
articulatory_notes: Tap or briefly trill the tongue tip before high-front 'i'. Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for unvoiced dental 't' and release into stressed central 'a'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k', then finish on true-mid rounded 'o'.
sound_symbolism: Bright 'ri' begins with a light tongue movement. Stressed 'ta' sets a broad dental beat in the centre, and final 'ko' answers with a deeper closure and rounded vowel. The word grows heavier from front to back.
usage_notes: Workload is usually mass-like, though separate intervals or arrangements may be compared. A source schedule can provide exact boundaries. 'riporu' is one task, 'naseru' an obligation, and 'pushali' the act of overloading a person or system beyond present capacity.

examples.translation: I do not accept this workload.

pillars.solarpunk-values: Workload makes maintenance, care, and coordination visible when a community decides how practical work is shared.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Separating workload from obligation and consent lets a person report what is being asked without conceding that the demand is fair or accepted.

semantic_domains.activity: the combined demand of work under stated conditions
semantic_domains.community: work distributed among people or groups
